segments.title: Why Jutland did not end the Royal Navy's battlecruiser strategy
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke challenges the idea that Jutland made battlecruisers obsolete and connects Britain's postwar capital-ship choices to worldwide commitments rather than a single North Sea battle.
segments.body: The losses at Jutland are presented as the result of cruisers being used in a battleship fight, not proof that speed and global reach had lost their value. Renown, Repulse, Hood, and the projected G3s followed because Britain still had to consider distant threats, imperial communications, and a future in which the German fleet no longer defined every capital-ship requirement.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts the familiar Jutland narrative with the Royal Navy's continuing battlecruiser construction and its postwar assessment of global threats.

segments.title: Hood as a global instrument of presence and deterrence
segments.summary: Hood's size, speed, armament, and public image made the ship a traveling demonstration of British power whose real purpose extended beyond winning a fleet engagement.
segments.body: A foreign observer could see a ship comparable to the strongest vessels in service and then be told that it was not Britain's most powerful capital ship. Dr. Clarke frames that contrast as conflict management: Hood encouraged rivals to imagine the strength held back at home and to reconsider the risks of confrontation.

evidence.note: The discussion describes Hood's visual impact and explicitly identifies deterrence and conflict management as the intended strategic effect.

segments.title: How G3 and N3 designs could disrupt rival procurement
segments.summary: The unconventional G3 battlecruisers and N3 battleships were intended to force rival navies to question their own designs, estimates, and building programs.
segments.body: Their unfamiliar layout advertised that the navy with the greatest recent capital-ship experience had reached different design conclusions. A fast G3 shown abroad could make the nominally slower, heavily armed N3 at home seem even more formidable, turning uncertainty about British performance into pressure on foreign procurement decisions.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains how the G3 and N3 designs' appearance, speed, guns, and uncertain published performance could force other navies to reassess construction.

segments.title: Why Nelson concentrated its main guns forward
segments.summary: The Nelson class's all-forward armament reduced the armored length while separating magazines from the hottest machinery spaces.
segments.body: Keeping the guns and magazines together forward and the engines aft made the armored citadel more compact, saving weight under the treaty displacement limit. The arrangement also kept boiler heat away from propellant and shells, while Nelson and Rodney accepted lower speed because Hood and the Renowns could cover the fast-capital-ship role.

evidence.note: The design explanation links magazine temperature, armored length, machinery placement, and the division of roles between Nelson, Hood, and the Renowns.

segments.title: Nelson's water-filled protection and full-scale torpedo test
segments.summary: Nelson and Rodney used layered air and buoyancy spaces that could be flooded in wartime, with trials indicating protection against a thousand-pound underwater charge.
segments.body: The internal bulge placed a protective bulkhead well inboard and used nearly 2,870 tons of water when the buoyancy spaces were flooded. A full-scale explosion tore a large opening in the outer plating but produced little leakage into the hull proper, supporting the conclusion that the ship could remain in action after a heavy torpedo hit; later results also led designers to seal ineffective venting plates.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke reads the dimensions, flooding arrangement, model trials, full-scale thousand-pound-charge result, and subsequent design changes.

segments.title: Why Nelson and Rodney were difficult opponents to disable
segments.summary: Nelson and Rodney traded speed for a dense armored citadel, making a direct engagement costly even for newer or faster capital ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes them as scaled-down N3s whose heavy guns and concentrated protection made opponents prefer avoidance, air attack, or submarine attack to a gun duel. Water carried for protection added roughly two thousand tons of defensive mass without counting toward standard displacement, while compact machinery freed still more weight for armor.

evidence.note: The analysis connects their battle-line role, concentrated armor, protected steering spaces, water weight, and the operational cost of engaging them.

segments.title: Why Nelson's water armour frustrated Admiral King
segments.summary: Admiral King learned of the British system after American wartime ship designs were largely fixed, leaving little opportunity to copy a protection scheme demonstrated against heavy torpedo charges.
segments.body: The irritation is explained as both practical and institutional. King faced a fleet already committed to existing construction while an ally revealed a mature protective system that American designers could no longer incorporate widely, and the discovery also challenged his conviction that the United States should lead in naval capability.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke links King's reaction to locked-in American designs, the demonstrated thousand-pound-charge protection, alliance expectations, and institutional pride.

segments.title: The design experience behind Britain's treaty battleships
segments.summary: Britain's treaty-era innovations drew on continuous battleship construction, experienced design teams, operational knowledge, and years of underwater-protection testing.
segments.body: The Nelson class was not an improvised response created only after the Washington Naval Treaty. Dr. Clarke traces its features through decades of British capital-ship work and the concentrated development that led from wartime experiments through the G3 and N3 studies, with the treaty limit imposing a new weight ceiling on an already mature body of engineering knowledge.

evidence.note: The discussion credits long production continuity, experienced designers and operators, and sustained G3/N3 and underwater-protection research before the treaty limit was applied.
